one_line_takeaway: On an emulator lacking bootloader/recovery/A-B-partition support, an LLM (PentestGPT) plus a custom script-generation pipeline fully automated 8 of 13 tested Android rooting/exploitation techniques (100% success, undetected) but could not exercise bootloader- or kernel-level exploitation at all, and RCE succeeded only on already-rooted devices.
key_finding: Of the tested features, most non-bootloader/non-kernel operations succeeded reliably: Backup Data, Sideload Magisk.zip, Reboot and Verify Root, Enable ADB over WiFi, Metasploit Exploit, ADB-Based Exploitation via Insecure Debugging, Network-Based MITM Exploitation, and Component Hijacking all achieved 100% success rate, were 'Not Detected' by security mechanisms, and scored Adaptability 3 (fully adaptable across all four tested Android configurations). Remote Code Execution via Malicious Software achieved only 50% success rate and 50% detection rate, Adaptability Score 2, and worked only on rooted devices (Android 11 and 12) -- it failed on the two unrooted configurations (Android 13, 14) because privilege elevation is blocked by Android security enforcement without root. Bootloader- and partition-dependent operations (Check Bootloader Status, Unlock Bootloader via Fastboot, Flash Custom Recovery/TWRP, Boot to TWRP, Patch boot.img with Magisk for A/B partitions) could not be tested/validated at all across any of the four configurations, and kernel exploits categorically 'Not Worked' on all four -- these are emulator platform limitations (Genymotion lacks Fastboot, a recovery partition, and A/B partition schemes), not demonstrated AI capability limits.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (not related work) is an end-to-end pipeline that automates Android rooting/privilege-escalation penetration testing using LLMs: (1) PentestGPT is queried with a structured rooting flowchart/prompt to produce a list of exploitation techniques (bootloader unlocking, kernel exploits, privilege escalation, etc.); (2) the authors' own custom Python/Streamlit web application, integrated with the OpenAI API, translates PentestGPT's natural-language output into runnable Bash/Python/ADB scripts, with a filtering step meant to strip dangerous/malicious commands; (3) the generated scripts are executed and validated on Genymotion Android emulator instances (both rooted and unrooted) across Android 11-14, with failures fed back to PentestGPT for iterative refinement. The authors then empirically evaluate which of these AI-generated exploit/rooting features actually work in the emulator and score each on four self-defined metrics (Success Rate, Security Detection Rate, Adaptability Score, Ethical Risk Factor).
threat_model: The paper is framed as an offensive-security/red-team automation study, not a study of a specific victim system's threat model against external attackers. The 'threat' explored is that an LLM (PentestGPT via OpenAI API) plus a script-generation web app could let an operator with access to an Android device (physical or emulated) automate rooting, privilege escalation, ADB-based exploitation, MITM network attacks, component hijacking, and RCE via malicious software, lowering the manual-expertise bar for these attacks. The authors explicitly discuss dual-use/misuse risk (an 'Ethical Risk Factor' metric per feature) and note their own script-generation app filters out commands it deems dangerous or malicious, plus require human review before execution -- i.e., they treat their own tool as something that must be gated against misuse, but they do not evaluate an adversary model beyond this self-imposed control.
limitations: Explicitly acknowledged by the authors: Genymotion's emulated environment lacks a Fastboot interface, recovery partition, and A/B partition scheme, so bootloader unlocking, custom recovery flashing, TWRP booting, and A/B boot.img patching could not be tested/confirmed on any Android version -- the authors state future work should use physical devices to evaluate these. Not stated by the authors but evident from the text: (1) no manual/human pentester baseline results are actually reported despite being promised in the abstract/intro framing as a comparison; (2) the paper does not report how many trials underlie each 'Success Rate' percentage (no attempt counts, confidence intervals, or statistical treatment) so 100%/50% figures cannot be interpreted for reliability; (3) 'Security Detection Rate' relies on the emulator's security mechanisms (SELinux, Play Protect, Verified Boot) which may behave differently than on physical hardware; (4) only a single LLM tool (PentestGPT, backed by OpenAI's API) is evaluated, so generality across other LLMs/agents is untested; (5) the authors are the same team that built and filtered the script-generation pipeline, so there is no independent/adversarial evaluation of whether the built-in ethical filtering actually prevents misuse.

datasets_used: No public dataset; empirical testbed of Genymotion Android emulator instances at Android 11 (rooted), Android 12 (rooted), Android 13 (unrooted), and Android 14 (unrooted)
quantitative_results: Backup Data: 100% success rate, Not Detected, Adaptability 3, High Risk; worked on all 4 tested configurations
Sideload Magisk.zip: 100% success rate, Not Detected, Adaptability 3, Medium Risk; worked on all 4 configurations
Reboot and Verify Root: 100% success rate, Not Detected, Adaptability 3, High Risk; worked on all 4 configurations
Enable ADB over WiFi: 100% success rate, Not Detected, Adaptability 3, High Risk; worked on all 4 configurations
Metasploit Exploit: 100% success rate, Not Detected, Adaptability 3, Low Risk; worked on all 4 configurations

Remote Code Execution (RCE) via Malicious Software: 50% success rate, 50% detection rate, Adaptability 2, Medium Risk; worked only on Android 11 and Android 12 (rooted), Not Worked on Android 13 and 14 (unrooted)
ADB-Based Exploitation via Insecure Debugging: 100% success rate, Not Detected, Adaptability 3, High Risk; worked on all 4 configurations
Network-Based Exploitation via MITM Attacks: 100% success rate, Not Detected, Adaptability 3, Low Risk; worked on all 4 configurations
Exploiting Android App Vulnerabilities (Component Hijacking): 100% success rate, Not Detected, Adaptability 3, Low Risk; worked on all 4 configurations
Use Kernel Exploits: Not Worked on all 4 configurations (Android 11/12/13/14)

Check Bootloader Status, Unlock Bootloader via Fastboot, Flash Custom Recovery (TWRP), Boot to TWRP, Patch boot.img with Magisk: unavailable/untestable on all 4 configurations due to emulator lacking Fastboot, recovery partition, and A/B partition scheme
baselines_compared: No manual/human-pentester baseline was actually run or reported in the results despite the paper's stated intent to compare automated vs. manual rooting; comparisons made are only across the four Android emulator configurations (rooted vs. unrooted, Android 11/12/13/14) and between General Prompts vs. Structured Prompts for querying PentestGPT (Fig. 4, not quantified in the tables)
